snippets.title: Medical Director Duties Checklist
snippets.body: Your medical director must: (1) be licensed in your state, (2) maintain a written supervisory agreement with your facility, (3) be available (in person or by telehealth) during operating hours for all medical-grade procedures, (4) review and approve protocols annually, (5) document oversight activities. Keep this agreement on file and accessible during inspections.

snippets.title: Who Can Perform What at Your Medspa
snippets.body: Injections (Botox, fillers) require a licensed physician, NP, or PA in most states. Laser treatments require state-specific licensing. Many states require a physician to be on-site or immediately available. Chemical peels above 30% strength are typically medical procedures. Estheticians can perform light chemical exfoliation, waxing, facials, and non-invasive treatments within their license scope. Always verify with your state's licensing board.

snippets.title: Informed Consent Requirements
snippets.body: For every medical-grade procedure, you need a signed informed consent form that: describes the procedure in plain language, lists common risks and side effects, discloses alternatives, and documents that the client had the opportunity to ask questions. Keep consents on file for at least 5 years. A verbal agreement alone is not sufficient.

snippets.title: Safe Language for Medspa Marketing
snippets.body: Avoid: 'FDA-approved' for off-label uses, specific outcome guarantees ('lose 3 inches'), clinical claims without evidence ('clinically proven to...'), use of MD or Doctor in your business name unless a licensed physician owns and operates the facility. Safe alternatives: 'FDA-cleared device,' 'many clients experience,' 'complements a healthy lifestyle,' 'performed by licensed providers.'

snippets.title: Cosmetology License Renewal Quick Reference
snippets.body: Most states require cosmetology license renewal every 1-2 years with continuing education hours. Typical CE topics: infection control, chemical safety, updated state laws. Check your state board's website for exact requirements. Never let a license expire. Working with an expired license is a disciplinary offense in every state and can result in fines or suspension.

snippets.title: Booth Rental Compliance Checklist
snippets.body: To maintain a valid independent contractor relationship with booth renters: (1) Have a written lease agreement with a fixed rental amount, (2) Do not control their schedule, pricing, or client list, (3) Do not require them to use specific products, (4) Provide separate scheduling and payment systems, (5) Do not include them in employee meetings or trainings as mandatory. Document everything.

snippets.title: Pedicure Basin Sanitation Protocol
snippets.body: After each client: drain, clean with soap and water, disinfect with EPA-registered disinfectant, run solution through jets for 10 minutes. At end of day: clean and disinfect again, leave dry. Weekly: remove jet covers and clean behind them. Keep a written log of every cleaning. Nail salon infections traced to dirty basins have resulted in permanent license revocations.

snippets.title: Social Media Photo Consent Best Practice
snippets.body: Before posting a client photo on Instagram, TikTok, or any social platform, get written consent. A simple signed or email-confirmed release stating the client approves use of their likeness for marketing is enough. Add an opt-in checkbox on your intake form. Using client photos without consent can result in complaints to your licensing board and civil claims for misappropriation of likeness.

snippets.title: Liability Waiver Best Practices
snippets.body: An enforceable gym waiver should: (1) be a standalone document, not buried in the membership contract, (2) use plain language describing specific risks, (3) specifically name 'negligence' if you want to waive negligence claims (required in most states), (4) be signed before any activity, not after, (5) be dated and retained for at least 3 years. Minors cannot sign their own waivers. A parent or guardian must sign.

snippets.title: Equipment Inspection Schedule
snippets.body: Cardio machines: inspect and lubricate monthly, professional service annually. Free weights: inspect for cracks, worn knurling, and collar integrity monthly. Cable machines: inspect cables for fraying weekly. Bench pads: check for tears and exposed foam weekly. Keep a maintenance log for every piece of equipment. An injury caused by equipment you knew was defective is nearly impossible to defend.

snippets.title: Personal Training Certification Requirements
snippets.body: Most states do not regulate personal trainer certifications, but your liability insurer likely requires trainers to hold a certification from an NCCA-accredited organization (such as NASM, ACE, ACSM, or NSCA). Verify this requirement with your insurer. If you employ trainers who are uncertified and a client is injured, you may be outside your policy coverage.

snippets.title: ADA Accessibility Basics for Gyms
snippets.body: Your facility must: provide accessible parking and entrance, ensure locker rooms have accessible stalls and changing areas, make at least one of each type of cardio and strength machine accessible or adaptable, ensure reception desks have a lowered section for wheelchair users. New construction must meet current ADA standards. For existing facilities, you must remove barriers where 'readily achievable.' An ADA audit every 2-3 years is good practice.

snippets.title: Overtime Rules Every Service Business Owner Needs to Know
snippets.body: Under the FLSA, non-exempt employees must receive 1.5x their regular pay for hours over 40 in a workweek. To be exempt (salaried, no overtime), employees must meet both a salary threshold (currently $684/week, rising to $1,128/week in 2027) AND qualify under a white-collar duties test. Most front-desk staff, technicians, and service providers are non-exempt regardless of whether you pay them salary.

snippets.title: New Hire Paperwork Checklist
snippets.body: For every new employee: (1) Completed I-9 employment eligibility verification within 3 days of start), (2) W-4 federal withholding form, (3) State tax withholding form, (4) Signed offer letter or employment agreement, (5) Signed handbook acknowledgment, (6) Direct deposit form if applicable. For contractors: W-9 form. File these in a secure, separate personnel file for each person.

snippets.title: Minimum Insurance Coverage for Service Businesses
snippets.body: At minimum, you should carry: (1) General liability ($1M per occurrence / $2M aggregate), (2) Professional liability / errors & omissions if you provide advice or recommendations, (3) Workers' compensation (required in almost every state once you have employees), (4) Commercial property, (5) Business interruption coverage. Review coverage amounts annually as your revenue grows.

snippets.title: How to Prepare for a State Inspection
snippets.body: Keep ready at all times: current facility license, all operator licenses in a binder, health department permit, most recent inspection report and your corrective actions, sanitation logs, chemical safety data sheets (SDS) for all products, first aid kit, and a posted emergency action plan. A well-organized 'inspection binder' is your best asset when an inspector walks in unannounced.

snippets.title: Do Medspas Need to Comply with HIPAA?
snippets.body: If your medspa provides medical services (injections, laser treatments, IV therapy, or anything requiring a medical history or prescription), you are likely a 'covered entity' or 'business associate' under HIPAA. This means: (1) Use HIPAA-compliant intake forms and record storage, (2) Train staff on PHI handling annually, (3) Have a Notice of Privacy Practices posted and signed by clients, (4) Report breaches within 60 days. Non-compliance fines start at $100 per violation.

snippets.title: Home-Based Salon Licensing Requirements
snippets.body: Operating a salon from your home requires: a home occupation permit from your city or county, a cosmetology establishment license (not just a personal license), compliance with local zoning (residential areas often restrict client traffic and signage), and separate entrance for clients in many jurisdictions. Check all three layers: state licensing board, local zoning, and health department permits.

snippets.title: Managing Tipped Employees at Fitness Facilities
snippets.body: If your gym includes a spa, snack bar, or service area where employees receive tips, federal law allows you to pay a tipped minimum wage (as low as $2.13/hour federally), but only if their tips bring total compensation to at least the regular minimum wage every week. You must make up the difference if tips fall short. Many states have eliminated the tipped minimum wage. Verify your state's rules.

snippets.title: Laser and IPL Device Operator Requirements by State
snippets.body: Laser and IPL (intense pulsed light) device requirements vary dramatically by state. Some states (TX, FL, CA) require that a physician be on-site or available during treatments. Others allow RNs, NPs, or even trained estheticians to operate certain devices. Before purchasing or using any energy-based device, confirm the operator requirements with your state medical board and cosmetology board; sometimes both have jurisdiction.

snippets.title: Chemical Safety Requirements for Nail Technicians
snippets.body: OSHA requires that all salons using hazardous chemicals maintain a Safety Data Sheet (SDS, formerly MSDS) for every product and make them accessible to all employees. Ventilation standards apply to nail salons. OSHA has specific guidance on methyl methacrylate (MMA) and acetone exposure limits. Train staff on proper ventilation, glove use, and chemical storage. Non-compliance can result in OSHA citations up to $16,131 per violation.

snippets.title: Gym Membership Agreement Essentials
snippets.body: A compliant gym membership agreement should include: (1) Clear pricing and billing cycle, (2) Cancellation policy with notice requirements, (3) Auto-renewal disclosure in bold type (required by many state consumer protection laws), (4) Refund policy, (5) Liability limitation clause, (6) Health risk acknowledgment. Many states have specific gym membership laws (CA, NY, FL, TX) with mandatory cooling-off periods and cancellation rights. Check your state's statute.

snippets.title: Client Data Privacy Basics
snippets.body: Even if you're not subject to HIPAA, you still have privacy obligations. Best practices: (1) Only collect data you actually need, (2) Use a secure, password-protected booking system, (3) Don't share client contact lists with third parties without consent, (4) If you're in California, Colorado, Virginia, or several other states, you have additional obligations under state privacy laws. Post a privacy policy on your website.

snippets.title: Annual Compliance Audit Checklist for Medspas
snippets.body: Every year, review: all provider licenses and medical director agreement, informed consent forms (update for any new procedures), HIPAA policies and employee training records, advertising materials for FTC compliance, equipment safety inspections and calibration records, adverse event log, infection control protocols, and your professional liability insurance. Schedule this for 60 days before your facility license renewal.

snippets.title: At-Will Employment Doesn't Mean Anything Goes
snippets.body: Even in at-will states, you cannot terminate employees for discriminatory reasons (protected class), in retaliation for a safety complaint or OSHA report, or in violation of an employment contract or employee handbook promise. Document performance issues in writing before termination. A wrongful termination claim is one of the most common legal risks for small service businesses.
